perturbation.original_text: HERC shall provide for ten (10) days notice to THC and HSI in the event of any modification, cancellation or termination.
perturbation.changed_text: HERC shall provide for one (1) day notice to THC and HSI in the event of any modification, cancellation or termination.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice period for modifications or cancellation of insurance policies to one day contradicts standard insurance regulations, which generally require a minimum of 10 days' notice to the insured and additional insured parties. This ensures sufficient time to secure alternative coverage and avoid lapses in protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 627.4133 requires a minimum notice period for cancellation or nonrenewal of insurance policies.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 627.4133(2)(a) requires at least 45 days' advance notice of nonrenewal and 20 days' notice of cancellation for property and casualty insurance policies. Reducing the contractual notice period to one day is inconsistent with this statutory requirement.

F.S. 627.4133 627.4133 Notice of cancellation, nonrenewal, or renewal premium. — (1) Except as provided in subsection (2): (a) An insurer issuing a policy providing coverage for workers’ compensation and employer’s liability insurance, property, casualty, except mortgage guaranty, surety, or marine insurance, other than motor vehicle insurance subject to s. 627.728 , shall give the first-named insured at least 45 days’ advance written notice of nonrenewal or of the renewal premium. If the policy is not to be renewed, the written notice shall state the reason or reasons as to why the policy is not to be renewed. This requirement applies only if the insured has furnished all of the necessary information so as to enable the insurer to develop the renewal premium prior to the expiration date of the policy to be renewed. (b) An insurer issuing a policy providing coverage for property, casualty, except mortgage guaranty, surety, or marine insurance, other than motor vehicle insurance subject to s. 627.728 or s. 627.7281 , shall give the first-named insured written notice of cancellation or termination other than nonrenewal at least 45 days prior to the effective date of the cancellation or termination, including in the written notice the reason or reasons for the cancellation or termination, except that: 1. When cancellation is for nonpayment of premium, at least 10 days’ written notice of cancellation accompanied by the reason therefor shall be given. As used in this subparagraph and s. 440.42 (3), the term “nonpayment of premium” means failure of the named insured to discharge when due any of her or his obligations in connection with the payment of premiums on a policy or any installment of such premium, whether the premium is payable directly to the insurer or its agent or indirectly under any premium finance plan or extension of credit, or failure to maintain membership in an organization if such membership is a condition precedent to insurance coverage. “Nonpayment of premium” al
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period for modifications or cancellation to one day, while Florida Statutes § 627.4133(2)(a) requires at least 45 days' advance notice of nonrenewal and 20 days' notice of cancellation for property and casualty insurance policies, creating a direct conflict with the statutory requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions "SECTION 4133 Notice of cancellation, nonrenewal, or renewal premium" and refers to F.S. 627.4133, confirming the law's relevance. Therefore, the reduction to one day constitutes a legal contradiction by failing to meet the legally mandated minimum notice period.

perturbation.original_text: If HERC has not cured any such breach within thirty (30) days after HERC receives such notice, this Agreement shall automatically terminate without further notice.
perturbation.changed_text: If HERC has not cured any such breach within ten (10) days after HERC receives such notice, this Agreement shall automatically terminate without further notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach to ten days may be considered commercially unreasonable in certain contexts, particularly for complex contractual obligations. This is because some jurisdictions mandate a reasonable opportunity to cure a breach, and ten days may be insufficient, potentially leading to legal challenges regarding wrongful termination.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law requires a 'reasonable time' to cure a breach of contract, varying based on the nature of the breach and the contract's complexity.
perturbation.law_citation: Filmline Productions, Inc. v. United Artists Corp., 662 N.Y.S.2d 9 (App. Div. 1997)
perturbation.law_explanation: Filmline Productions, Inc. v. United Artists Corp. establishes that New York law (which governs this contract) implies a requirement for a 'reasonable time' to cure a breach. Ten days may be deemed unreasonable depending on the severity and nature of the breach, potentially conflicting with established legal precedent.
perturbation.justification: The original contract provided thirty days for cure, whereas the changed contract reduces this period to ten days. According to the perturbation explanation, jurisdictions typically require a reasonable cure period. The law explanation further cites 'Filmline Productions, Inc. v. United Artists Corp.' which indicates that New York law implies a requirement for a 'reasonable time' to cure a breach. Because no snippets are provided, we must rely on the Perturbation and Law explanations. A ten-day cure period may be deemed unreasonable for certain breaches, potentially contradicting the implied legal requirement for a reasonable time to cure, as explained in the Law Explanation. This constitutes a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notices are deemed properly given as follows: (a) if delivered personally, on the date delivered, (b) if delivered by a commercial overnight courier, one (1) business day after such notice is sent, and (c) if delivered by facsimile or electronic mail, on the date of transmission, with confirmation of transmission; provided, however, that if the notice is sent by facsimile or electronic mail, the notice must be followed by a copy of the notice being delivered by a means provided in (a) or (b):
perturbation.changed_text: Notices are deemed properly given as follows: (a) if delivered personally, on the date delivered, (b) if delivered by a commercial overnight courier, five (5) business days after such notice is sent, and (c) if delivered by facsimile or electronic mail, five (5) days after the date of transmission, with confirmation of transmission; provided, however, that if the notice is sent by facsimile or electronic mail, the notice must NOT be followed by a copy of the notice being delivered by a means provided in (a) or (b):
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice period delivered by a commercial overnight courier to five business days is considered unreasonable, as courier services guarantee much faster delivery, typically within 1-2 business days. Furthermore, eliminating the requirement for a follow-up hard copy for emailed/faxed notices makes it more likely that these notices will go unnoticed and cause legal issues in the future.
perturbation.contradicted_law: FRCP 5(b)(2)(E) governs service by electronic means and allows for specific methods and timing.
perturbation.law_explanation: While FRCP 5(b)(2)(E) doesn't explicitly mandate a hard copy follow-up for electronic service, eliminating such a requirement can create ambiguity and disputes regarding effective notice, especially if there's a failure in electronic transmission or receipt. Courts generally prefer clear and unambiguous proof of service, which a follow-up hard copy can provide. The five-day period for courier delivery could be considered unreasonable by a court given the ready availability of faster delivery methods.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 5. Serving and Filing Pleadings and Other Papers | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 5. Serving and Filing Pleadings and Other Papers (a) Service: When Required. (1) In General. Unless these rules provide otherwise, each of the following papers must be served on every party: (A) an order stating that service is required; (B) a pleading filed after the original complaint, unless the court orders otherwise under Rule 5(c) because there are numerous defendants; (C) a discovery paper required to be served on a party, unless the court orders otherwise; (D) a written motion, except one that may be heard ex parte; and (E) a written notice, appearance, demand, or offer of judgment, or any similar paper. (2) If a Party Fails to Appear. No service is required on a party who is in default for failing to appear. But a pleading that asserts a new claim for relief against such a party must be served on that party under Rule 4 . (3) Seizing Property. If an action is begun by seizing property and no person is or need be named as a defendant, any service required before the filing of an appearance, answer, or claim must be made on the person who had custody or possession of the property when it was seized. (b) Service: How Made. (1) Serving an Attorney. If a party is represented by an attorney, service under this rule must be made on the attorney unless the court orders service on the party. (2) Service in General. A paper is served under this rule by: (A) handing it to the person; (B) leaving it: (i) at the person's office with a clerk or other person in charge or, if no one is in charge, in a conspicuous place in the office; or (ii) if the person has no office or the office is closed, at the person's dwelling or usual place of abode with someone of suitable age and discretion who resides there; (C) mailing it to the person's last known address—in which event service is complete upon mailing; (D) leaving it with the court clerk if the person has no known address; (E) sending it to a registered user by filing it with the court's electronic-filing system or sending it by other electronic means that the person consented to in writing—in either of which events service is complete upon filing or sending, but is not effective if the filer or sender learns that it did not reach the person to be served; or (F) delivering it by any other means that the person consented to in writing—in which event service is complete when the person making service delivers it to the agency designated to make delivery. (3) Using Court Facilities. [Abrogated (Apr._, 2018, eff. Dec. 1, 2018)] (c) Serving Numerous Defendants. (1) In General. If an action involves an unusually large number of defendants,

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces inconsistencies in the notice period and eliminates the hard copy follow-up, creating a potential contradiction. The original text specifies a one-business-day delivery timeframe for overnight couriers, reflecting the standard practice. The changed text extends this to five business days, which is unreasonable and inconsistent with common courier delivery times. Furthermore, eliminating the hard copy requirement for electronic notices increases the risk of non-receipt or disputes. Although the scraped snippets do not explicitly discuss delivery time frames for overnight couriers, snippet 1 states that service can be made via electronic means if the person has consented to it in writing, but is not effective if the filer or sender learns that it did not reach the person to be served. The removal of the hard copy makes verifying receipt more difficult and contradicts the snippet's implicit expectation that service should be effective.
